title: SEGMENT 13 — The $1000 genome, the $200 genome: what the cost trajectory tells us

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — The $1000 genome, the $200 genome: what the cost trajectory tells us
Let me come back to the cost story, because it's worth dwelling on.

The dollar-per-genome trajectory of Illumina-driven sequencing is, by any reasonable measure, the most dramatic price reduction in the history of biotechnology, and it's worth understanding what drove it and what it means.

In 2001, when the first draft of the human genome was published, the total cost of that project — when you include the international collaboration, the parallel private effort by Celera, the years of labor, the infrastructure — was estimated at around three billion dollars for that single genome.
By 2007, when next-generation sequencing was just starting to take hold, the per-genome cost had dropped to maybe ten million dollars.
By 2010, with the first generation of Illumina HiSeq instruments in widespread use, it had dropped to about a hundred thousand dollars.

By 2014, with the HiSeq X Ten, Illumina officially announced the $1000 genome — for the first time, the cost of sequencing a human genome at the standard 30x coverage was a thousand dollars in reagents, on the assumption that you ran the instrument at full utilization on a high-throughput project.
By 2022, with the launch of the NovaSeq X, that cost had dropped further to about two hundred dollars per genome.
Two hundred dollars.
Less than the cost of a typical clinical blood panel.
Less than a fancy dinner for two.
Cheaper than a lot of consumer electronics.
And what you get for two hundred dollars is the complete, three-billion-base-pair sequence of one human being.
That fact, by itself, is one of the most extraordinary things in modern biology.
Here's the analogy that makes the cost trajectory feel real.
In 2003, sequencing a human genome cost about as much as building a hundred-million-dollar pharmaceutical R&D program.
In 2014, it cost about as much as a high-end laptop.
In 2024, it costs about as much as a nice meal out.
That's the trajectory.

Imagine if any other technology had dropped fifteen million-fold in two decades — if airline tickets had gone from three thousand dollars to fraction of a penny — and you can get a sense of how disruptive this has been.
What drove it?
Several things, in roughly equal measure.
First, Moore's-law-like improvements in the optics: higher-resolution cameras, denser pixel arrays, faster image processing.

Second, advances in surface chemistry that allowed denser cluster packing on flow cells — going from a few million clusters per square millimeter in the early days to billions per square millimeter now.
Third, improvements in the reversible terminator chemistry itself: faster reactions, more efficient incorporation, better dyes.
Fourth, scaling effects on the instruments: bigger flow cells, parallelized imaging, more cycles per run.
Fifth, the engineering of the reagents themselves: economies of scale, better synthesis methods, cheaper enzymes.
No single factor accounts for the cost reduction; it's the product of many factors compounded over fifteen years of intense engineering.
What does the cheap genome enable?
The list is long.

Population-scale genomics: projects like the UK Biobank, which has sequenced half a million genomes; the All of Us project in the US; gnomAD, the genome aggregation database that has collated over 100,000 exomes and tens of thousands of genomes to map human genetic variation.
None of these projects could have been done at 2010 prices.

Clinical genomics at scale: routine exome sequencing for rare disease diagnosis is now standard of care in many healthcare systems, with thousands of new diagnoses per year that would otherwise have been impossible.
Cancer genomics: tumor sequencing for treatment selection is now routine, with panels of dozens to hundreds of genes screened for actionable mutations in most major cancer centers.
Pathogen surveillance: during the COVID-19 pandemic, the global community sequenced millions of SARS-CoV-2 genomes and used that data to track variants in near real time.
None of this would have been remotely feasible at pre-NGS prices.
And there's a deeper point.
At a thousand dollars per genome, sequencing was something you considered carefully — a major investment for any specific patient or project.
At two hundred dollars per genome, sequencing is becoming something you do reflexively — like ordering a CBC or a metabolic panel, just one more test in the workup.
The mental model shifts from "should we sequence this?
" to "sure, sequence it, the data will be useful for something.
" That shift, more than any specific clinical application, is what cheap sequencing means for the long-term role of genomics in medicine.
We're moving from sequencing as a precious resource to sequencing as a commodity.
The current target Illumina has discussed publicly is the hundred-dollar genome, which the NovaSeq X is approaching for high-volume customers.
After that, the next milestone is the fifty-dollar genome, and after that, maybe ten dollars.
There's no obvious physical limit yet.
The chemistry isn't anywhere near saturated.
The optics aren't near their theoretical limit.
The flow cells could in principle be packed more densely.
Whether competition forces the price down faster, or Illumina chooses to harvest margin and let prices stabilize, is one of the more interesting business questions in the field right now.
